name: config
context: Kubeconfig files, cluster contexts, authentication methods, and namespace configuration for connecting to Kubernetes clusters
info: Kubernetes configuration governs how clients (kubectl, SDKs, CI/CD pipelines) connect to clusters. The kubeconfig file (~/.kube/config) defines clusters (API server URLs, CA certs), users (auth credentials: tokens, client certs, OIDC, exec-based), and contexts (cluster + user + namespace combinations). Agents need to understand kubeconfig to switch between clusters (dev, staging, prod), set default namespaces, manage credentials securely, and configure CI/CD pipelines. This node covers kubeconfig structure, context management, authentication methods (service accounts, OIDC, cloud provider auth), namespace configuration, and multi-cluster workflows. See [[kubernetes/kubectl]] for the CLI commands that use this configuration.
body:
# Kubernetes Configuration

All Kubernetes client access flows through a kubeconfig file. This file tells kubectl (and any K8s SDK) which cluster to talk to, how to authenticate, and what namespace to default to.

## Kubeconfig File Structure

Default location: `~/.kube/config`. Override with `$KUBECONFIG` or `--kubeconfig` flag.

```yaml
apiVersion: v1
kind: Config
current-context: production

clusters:
  - name: production
    cluster:
      server: https://api.prod.example.com:6443
      certificate-authority-data: <base64-ca-cert>
  - name: staging
    cluster:
      server: https://api.staging.example.com:6443
      certificate-authority-data: <base64-ca-cert>
  - name: local
    cluster:
      server: https://127.0.0.1:6443
      insecure-skip-tls-verify: true

users:
  - name: admin
    user:
      client-certificate-data: <base64-client-cert>
      client-key-data: <base64-client-key>
  - name: developer
    user:
      token: <bearer-token>
  - name: oidc-user
    user:
      exec:
        apiVersion: client.authentication.k8s.io/v1beta1
        command: kubelogin
        args: ["get-token", "--oidc-issuer-url=https://login.example.com"]

contexts:
  - name: production
    context:
      cluster: production
      user: admin
      namespace: default
  - name: staging
    context:
      cluster: staging
      user: developer
      namespace: staging-app
  - name: local-dev
    context:
      cluster: local
      user: admin
      namespace: dev
```

## Context Management

A context binds a cluster + user + namespace into a named profile:

```bash
# List all contexts
kubectl config get-contexts

# Show current context
kubectl config current-context

# Switch context
kubectl config use-context staging

# Set default namespace for current context
kubectl config set-context --current --namespace=my-app

# Create a new context
kubectl config set-context my-context \
  --cluster=production \
  --user=admin \
  --namespace=my-team

# Delete a context
kubectl config delete-context old-context

# Rename a context
kubectl config rename-context old-name new-name
```

### Fast Switching with kubectx/kubens

```bash
# Install
brew install kubectx

# Switch context interactively
kubectx
kubectx production  # switch directly
kubectx -           # switch to previous

# Switch namespace
kubens
kubens my-app       # switch directly
kubens -            # switch to previous
```

## Authentication Methods

### 1. Bearer Token (ServiceAccount)

Used by in-cluster Pods and CI/CD pipelines:

```bash
# Create a ServiceAccount
kubectl create serviceaccount deploy-bot -n production

# Create a long-lived token (k8s 1.24+)
kubectl create token deploy-bot -n production --duration=8760h

# Or create a Secret-bound token (older clusters)
kubectl apply -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: v1
kind: Secret
metadata:
  name: deploy-bot-token
  namespace: production
  annotations:
    kubernetes.io/service-account.name: deploy-bot
type: kubernetes.io/service-account-token
EOF

# Extract token
kubectl get secret deploy-bot-token -n production -o jsonpath='{.data.token}' | base64 -d
```

Set in kubeconfig:
```yaml
users:
  - name: deploy-bot
    user:
      token: <the-token>
```

### 2. Client Certificate

Used by cluster admins, generated during cluster setup:

```yaml
users:
  - name: admin
    user:
      client-certificate-data: <base64>
      client-key-data: <base64>
```

### 3. OIDC / Cloud Provider Auth

Cloud-managed clusters use provider-specific auth plugins:

```bash
# AWS EKS
aws eks update-kubeconfig --name my-cluster --region us-east-1
# Uses aws-iam-authenticator or aws eks get-token under the hood

# GCP GKE
gcloud container clusters get-credentials my-cluster --zone us-central1-a
# Uses gcloud for auth

# Azure AKS
az aks get-credentials --resource-group my-rg --name my-cluster
# Uses kubelogin for Azure AD
```

### 4. Exec-based Auth

Dynamic token generation via external commands:

```yaml
users:
  - name: oidc-user
    user:
      exec:
        apiVersion: client.authentication.k8s.io/v1beta1
        command: kubelogin
        args:
          - get-token
          - --oidc-issuer-url=https://login.example.com
          - --oidc-client-id=kubectl
        interactiveMode: IfAvailable
```

## Merging Multiple Kubeconfigs

When managing multiple clusters, merge configs:

```bash
# Set KUBECONFIG to multiple files (colon-separated on Linux/macOS)
export KUBECONFIG=~/.kube/config:~/.kube/staging.yaml:~/.kube/production.yaml

# Verify all contexts are available
kubectl config get-contexts

# Permanently merge into one file
KUBECONFIG=~/.kube/config:~/.kube/new-cluster.yaml kubectl config view --flatten > ~/.kube/merged
mv ~/.kube/merged ~/.kube/config
```

## In-Cluster Configuration

Pods running inside a cluster automatically get a ServiceAccount token mounted at `/var/run/secrets/kubernetes.io/serviceaccount/`:

```bash
# Inside a Pod
TOKEN=$(cat /var/run/secrets/kubernetes.io/serviceaccount/token)
CA_CERT=/var/run/secrets/kubernetes.io/serviceaccount/ca.crt
API_SERVER=https://kubernetes.default.svc

# Direct API call
curl -s --cacert $CA_CERT -H "Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN" \
  $API_SERVER/api/v1/namespaces/default/pods
```

Kubernetes SDKs auto-detect in-cluster config:
```python
# Python (kubernetes client)
from kubernetes import client, config
config.load_incluster_config()  # auto-detects in-cluster
v1 = client.CoreV1Api()
```

```javascript
// Node.js (@kubernetes/client-node)
const k8s = require('@kubernetes/client-node');
const kc = new k8s.KubeConfig();
kc.loadFromCluster(); // auto-detects in-cluster
```

## Namespace Configuration

Namespaces isolate resources within a cluster:

```bash
# List namespaces
kubectl get namespaces

# Create a namespace
kubectl create namespace my-team

# Set default namespace for kubectl
kubectl config set-context --current --namespace=my-team

# All subsequent commands target my-team:
kubectl get pods  # same as: kubectl get pods -n my-team
```

See [[kubernetes/namespaces]] for namespace isolation, resource quotas, and network policies.

## CI/CD Pipeline Configuration

```bash
# Set up kubectl in a CI pipeline
# 1. Store kubeconfig or token as CI secret
# 2. Configure kubectl
export KUBECONFIG=$(mktemp)
kubectl config set-cluster production \
  --server=https://api.prod.example.com:6443 \
  --certificate-authority=/path/to/ca.crt
kubectl config set-credentials deployer \
  --token=$KUBE_TOKEN
kubectl config set-context deploy \
  --cluster=production \
  --user=deployer \
  --namespace=production
kubectl config use-context deploy

# 3. Deploy
kubectl apply -f ./k8s/
kubectl rollout status deployment/my-app
```

## Security Best Practices

1. **Never commit kubeconfig** — add `*.kubeconfig` and `.kube/` to `.gitignore`
2. **Use short-lived tokens** — prefer `kubectl create token` over long-lived Secrets
3. **Scope ServiceAccounts** — bind minimal [[kubernetes/rbac]] Roles per ServiceAccount
4. **Rotate credentials** — regenerate tokens and certificates on schedule
5. **Use exec-based auth** — OIDC/cloud auth refreshes automatically
6. **Separate kubeconfigs** — don't mix prod and dev in one file

## Environment Variables

| Variable | Purpose |
|----------|----------|
| `KUBECONFIG` | Path to kubeconfig file(s), colon-separated (default: `~/.kube/config`) |
| `KUBE_CONTEXT` | Override active context name |
| `KUBE_NAMESPACE` | Default namespace for operations |
| `KUBERNETES_SERVICE_HOST` | API server host (set automatically in-cluster) |
| `KUBERNETES_SERVICE_PORT` | API server port (set automatically in-cluster) |

sections.title: Kubeconfig file structure
sections.body: A kubeconfig has four top-level keys: `clusters` (API server endpoints + CA certs), `users` (auth credentials), `contexts` (cluster + user + namespace tuples), and `current-context` (the active context). Each cluster entry has `server` (URL) and `certificate-authority-data` or `certificate-authority` (path). Each user entry has auth credentials (token, client cert/key, exec command). Each context binds a cluster name to a user name and optionally a default namespace.

sections.title: Authentication methods
sections.body: Four primary auth methods: **Bearer tokens** — ServiceAccount tokens (short-lived via `kubectl create token` or Secret-bound). **Client certificates** — x509 certs generated during cluster setup. **OIDC** — federated identity via OpenID Connect (Azure AD, Google, Okta). **Exec-based** — external command generates token dynamically (aws-iam-authenticator, gcloud, kubelogin). Cloud providers (EKS, GKE, AKS) use exec-based auth by default. In-cluster Pods get automatic ServiceAccount tokens at `/var/run/secrets/kubernetes.io/serviceaccount/token`.

sections.title: Multi-cluster configuration
sections.body: Manage multiple clusters by: 1) Multiple contexts in one kubeconfig, 2) Separate kubeconfig files merged via `KUBECONFIG=file1:file2`, 3) Per-command override with `--kubeconfig` flag. Use `kubectx` for fast interactive switching. In CI/CD, build kubeconfig dynamically per environment. For multi-cluster tools (Argo CD, Rancher, Crossplane), register clusters separately.

sections.title: In-cluster configuration
sections.body: Pods running inside a Kubernetes cluster have automatic access to the API server. The ServiceAccount token, CA cert, and namespace are mounted at `/var/run/secrets/kubernetes.io/serviceaccount/`. Client libraries detect this automatically: Python's `config.load_incluster_config()`, Go's `rest.InClusterConfig()`, Node's `kc.loadFromCluster()`. The API server is reachable at `https://kubernetes.default.svc`. No kubeconfig file is needed. The Pod's ServiceAccount determines [[kubernetes/rbac]] permissions.

sections.title: Security best practices
sections.body: Never commit kubeconfig files to version control. Use short-lived tokens (`kubectl create token`) instead of long-lived Secret-based tokens. Scope ServiceAccounts with minimal [[kubernetes/rbac]] Roles. Use exec-based auth (OIDC, cloud provider) for automatic credential refresh. Separate kubeconfig files per environment. In CI/CD, use dedicated ServiceAccounts with scoped permissions, not admin credentials. Rotate client certificates and tokens on schedule.

links.context: kubectl uses kubeconfig to connect to clusters
RBAC controls what authenticated users and ServiceAccounts can do
Namespaces scope kubectl commands and resource isolation
Connection and auth failures are common troubleshooting targets

related.note: kubectl is the primary consumer of kubeconfig
RBAC policies control what authenticated identities can do in the cluster
Contexts can specify a default namespace

frontmatter.author: dojo-community
frontmatter.audience: developers, DevOps engineers, SREs, AI agents
